disease.name: Pressure Sore / Pressure Ulcer
disease.note: Localized injury to skin/underlying tissue due to prolonged pressure. Common over bony prominences. Graded I-IV by NPUAP. In Kenyan hospitals, prolonged bed occupancy, shortage of pressure-relieving mattresses, and lack of turning protocols contribute to high prevalence. Sacral sores are most common in the elderly and spinal cord injury patients.
presentingComplaints: Sore or wound at the tailbone or heel
Skin breakdown from lying in bed
Non-healing wound over a bony area
Painful red area on the back or buttocks

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Grade III-IV/deep infection/osteomyelitis -> SEVERE; Grade I-II -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: How long have you been bedridden or in a wheelchair?
Where exactly is the wound located?
When did the skin breakdown start?
Do you have loss of sensation in that area?
Are you incontinent of urine or stool?
Do you have diabetes?
Is the wound painful?
Have you had a pressure ulcer before?
What is your nutritional intake like?
Do you have a fever?
Is there any discharge from the wound? What color and smell?
Are you able to change position in bed by yourself?
Do you have any special mattress or cushion?
Are you on any steroids or chemotherapy?
Have you had any previous surgery for pressure sores?
Do you smoke?
How is your appetite?
Have you lost weight recently?
What is the patient's mobility status? Bedbound, chairbound, or ambulatory?
How often is the patient being turned or repositioned?
Is the patient incontinent of urine, stool, or both?
What is the patient's nutritional status? Any weight loss or poor appetite?
Does the patient have diabetes and what is their blood glucose control?
Does the patient have HIV or any other immunosuppressive condition?
Is the patient on steroids or immunosuppressive medications?
When was the ulcer first noticed and how has it changed?
Is the ulcer painful? Describe the quality and severity of pain
Has the patient had any previous pressure ulcers? Where and how treated?
Is there any drainage from the wound? Color, amount, and odor?
Has the patient had any fevers or chills recently?
What type of bed surface is the patient on (standard, foam, alternating pressure)?
Does the patient have any contractures or fixed postures?
Has there been any previous surgery for pressure ulcers?
Does the patient smoke cigarettes or use tobacco?
What is the patient's mental status? Can they follow commands?
Who is the primary caregiver? Are they trained in turning and wound care?
Is there a history of MRSA or multidrug-resistant organism colonization?
Does the patient have a DNR or do-not-hospitalize order influencing goals of care?

examination_findings.finding: Non-blanchable erythema over a bony prominence (Grade I)
Partial-thickness skin loss with visible dermis (Grade II)
Full-thickness skin loss with visible adipose tissue (Grade III)
Full-thickness tissue loss with exposed bone, tendon, or muscle (Grade IV)
Necrotic eschar or slough in wound bed
Undermining or tunneling
Periwound maceration
Signs of infection: purulent discharge, foul odor, erythema
Exposed bone (suspect osteomyelitis)
Surrounding cellulitis
Fever
Malnutrition signs: muscle wasting, low BMI
Assessment using Braden Scale for risk
Wound dimensions (length, width, depth) and photography
Pain assessment at wound site
Inspect ALL bony prominences: sacrum, coccyx, heels, trochanters, ischial tuberosities, occiput, scapulae, elbows, malleoli
Assess skin color over bony prominences for non-blanchable erythema
Perform blanching test with fingertip pressure for 3 seconds
Measure ulcer dimensions: length, width, and depth in cm
Assess for undermining (wound edge extends under intact skin)
Assess for tunneling (narrow tract from wound into deeper tissue)
Describe wound bed: percentage granulation, slough, and eschar
Assess exudate: type, amount, and odor
Inspect periwound skin for maceration, erythema, or induration
Palpate wound base for firmness or fluctuance
Assess wound pain using pain scale (0-10) at rest and during dressing changes
Evaluate range of motion of adjacent joints
Test light touch and pressure sensation around the wound
Perform probe-to-bone test with sterile metal probe
Assess for signs of systemic infection: fever, tachycardia, hypotension
Evaluate nutritional status: skin turgor, muscle wasting, fat loss
Assess patient's ability to reposition independently in bed
Evaluate support surface: mattress type, condition, presence of overlay
Photograph wound with measurement scale and color card
Assess for deep tissue injury: purple/maroon intact skin or blood-filled blister

bedside_scores.name: Braden Scale for Pressure Sore Risk
bedside_scores.interpretation: <=9: very high risk; 10-12: high risk; 13-14: moderate risk; 15-18: mild risk; 19-23: no risk.

bedside_scores.name: NPUAP Pressure Ulcer Staging
bedside_scores.interpretation: Staging guides management. Stage I-II: conservative. Stage III-IV: surgical debridement.

subtypes.name: Grade I-II Pressure Ulcer
subtypes.management: Pressure relief (turning every 2h, special mattress). Moisture management. Nutritional support. Barrier creams. Regular repositioning.

subtypes.name: Grade III-IV Pressure Ulcer
subtypes.management: Surgical debridement (sharp or surgical). Wound culture and antibiotics. NPWT. Pressure redistribution. Nutritional optimization. Reconstructive surgery (flap) for definitive closure.

subtypes.name: Unstageable Pressure Ulcer
subtypes.management: Debridement (sharp, enzymatic, or maggot therapy). Once base visible, re-stage and treat accordingly.

subtypes.name: Deep Tissue Injury
subtypes.management: Immediate and sustained pressure offloading. DO NOT massage. Monitor daily for progression. Debride when necrosis demarcates.

resuscitation: If septic: IV fluids, blood cultures, IV antibiotics
analgesia: Pain management during dressing changes (Paracetamol, Morphine PRN)
antibiotics: Only if signs of infection or osteomyelitis

grade_iii_iv.step1: Sharp surgical debridement of all necrotic tissue
grade_iii_iv.step7: Treat osteomyelitis if present (6 weeks IV antibiotics)

general: Pressure offloading post-flap (strict nil-by-sitting for 2-3 weeks)

pathways.criteria: intact skin or partial thickness, no deep necrosis
pathways.treatment: Pressure redistribution (q2h turning, special mattress). Moisture barrier. Nutritional support. Wound dressings (hydrocolloid, foam). No debridement needed.

pathways.criteria: full thickness, deep necrosis, exposed bone
pathways.treatment: Surgical debridement. NPWT. Antibiotics. Osteomyelitis treatment. Nutritional optimization. Reconstructive surgery (flap coverage). Pressure redistribution.

operative_templates.procedure: Sacral Pressure Sore Debridement and Flap Reconstruction
operative_templates.indication: Grade III-IV sacral pressure ulcer not healing with conservative management
operative_templates.approach: Excision and reconstruction with gluteal fasciocutaneous or myocutaneous flap
operative_templates.incision: Elliptical excision of ulcer including all necrotic tissue
steps: Excise ulcer and surrounding scar tissue en bloc
Remove underlying bursa and debride bone if osteomyelitic

watchFor: erythema over other bony prominences (contralateral, heels)
skin breakdown at same or adjacent site

dischargeCriteria: Wound clean with healthy granulation or flap viable
Patient/carer trained in turning and pressure relief
Follow-up arranged with wound care team

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: A pressure sore is an area of damaged skin caused by lying or sitting in one position for too long. The pressure cuts off blood supply to the skin, leading to breakdown.
patient_education.warningSigns: Spreading redness, fever, foul-smelling discharge, increasing pain, blackening of the wound.
patient_education.activity: Change position every 2 hours in bed. Use pillows to offload bony areas. Special mattress or cushion is essential.
patient_education.diet: High protein diet (meat, eggs, fish, beans) is essential for wound healing. Drink plenty of water. Nutritional supplements may be prescribed.
patient_education.medication: Take prescribed antibiotics if infected. Pain medication before dressing changes.
patient_education.followUp: Regular wound care follow-up. Weekly initially, then monthly as healing progresses.

before_discharge: Patient and family trained in turning and pressure relief
Home nursing or community health worker involvement
when_to_return: Wound increasing in size or depth

pathophysiologyDetail: Pressure ulcers (also known as decubitus ulcers or bedsores) result from unrelieved pressure over bony prominences that exceeds capillary perfusion pressure (normally 12-32 mmHg), leading to ischemia, tissue hypoxia, and ultimately cell necrosis. The classic mechanism involves three contributing factors: (1) direct pressure causing capillary occlusion and tissue ischemia; (2) shear stress when the skin remains stationary while deep tissues and bone move, causing angulation and occlusion of perforating vessels; and (3) friction causing superficial epidermal loss that predisposes to deeper injury. Moisture from incontinence, perspiration, or wound drainage macerates the skin and reduces its tolerance to pressure by 5-fold. The critical relationship is described by the pressure-time curve: high pressure for a short duration (e.g., 100 mmHg for 2 hours) or low pressure for a prolonged duration (e.g., 40 mmHg for 8 hours) both produce equivalent tissue damage. This explains why turning every 2 hours (the standard nursing intervention) prevents ulcer formation: it limits ischemia to less than the threshold needed to cause irreversible cell death.

The NPUAP/EPUAP international classification system grades pressure ulcers from I to IV, with two additional categories for unstageable and deep tissue injury. Stage I: non-blanchable erythema of intact skin — the earliest detectable sign. In darkly pigmented skin (common in African patients), Stage I may appear as persistent red, blue, or purple discoloration, warmth, edema, or induration rather than erythema — the key is that the color change does not blanch with fingertip pressure. Stage II: partial-thickness skin loss with exposed dermis, presenting as a shallow open ulcer with a red-pink wound bed, without slough or bruising. Stage III: full-thickness skin loss extending through the dermis into subcutaneous tissue but not through the underlying fascia. The ulcer appears as a deep crater with possible undermining or tunneling, and slough may be present. Stage IV: full-thickness skin and tissue loss with exposed or directly palpable fascia, muscle, tendon, ligament, cartilage, or bone. Osteomyelitis is a common complication of Stage IV ulcers. Unstageable: the base of the ulcer is covered by slough (yellow, tan, gray, green, or brown) or eschar (tan, brown, or black) that obscures the true depth. Deep Tissue Injury: a purple or maroon localized area of discolored intact skin or a blood-filled blister due to damage of underlying soft tissue from pressure and/or shear.

In Kenyan healthcare settings, pressure ulcers represent a significant quality-of-care indicator with high prevalence estimates (8-25% of hospitalized patients in some series, compared to 5-10% in high-resource settings). Contributing factors specific to the East African context include: (1) inadequate nurse-to-patient ratios (as low as 1:30-40 in some public hospitals), preventing 2-hourly turning; (2) shortage of pressure-relieving equipment (alternating pressure mattresses, heel protectors, positioning aids); (3) high prevalence of risk factors: HIV/AIDS with cachexia, spinal cord injury from trauma (boda-boda accidents), stroke-related immobility, and malnutrition; (4) lack of formal wound care teams and pressure ulcer prevention protocols; and (5) cultural practices that may prolong immobility (belief in 'complete bed rest' after illness). The sacrum is the most common site (40-50%), followed by heels (15-20%), trochanters (10-15%), and ischial tuberosities (10-15%).

landmarkTrials.trial: PRESSURE 2 Trial (Alternating Pressure Mattresses vs Alternating Pressure Overlays)
landmarkTrials.population: 2,029 hospitalized patients at high risk of pressure ulcers across 42 UK hospitals
landmarkTrials.intervention: Alternating pressure mattress (APM) vs alternating pressure overlay (APO)
landmarkTrials.outcome: No significant difference in ulcer incidence (8.7% vs 9.2%); both effective for prevention
landmarkTrials.impact: Lower-cost APO (approximately KES 15,000-30,000) is as effective as full APM (KES 100,000-300,000) for prevention
landmarkTrials.reference: Nixon J, et al. Alternating pressure mattresses versus alternating pressure overlays for pressure ulcer prevention. Lancet. 2019;394(10205):1206-1215.

landmarkTrials.trial: Nutritional Supplementation for Pressure Ulcer Healing (Systematic Review)
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 15 RCTs (1,200 patients) evaluating nutritional interventions
landmarkTrials.intervention: High-protein, arginine-, and micronutrient-enriched nutritional supplements vs standard diet
landmarkTrials.outcome: Reduced ulcer size by 25-40% more than controls at 8 weeks; higher healing rates with protein >1.5 g/kg/day
landmarkTrials.impact: Nutritional optimization is fundamental to pressure ulcer healing; affordable intervention in Kenyan setting (high-protein local foods: beans, eggs, fish)
landmarkTrials.reference: Smith ME, et al. Nutritional supplementation for pressure ulcer healing: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Wound Repair Regen. 2018;26(5):381-390.

landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 8 RCTs (692 patients) with Stage III-IV pressure ulcers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Negative pressure wound therapy (NPWT) vs conventional wound dressings
landmarkTrials.outcome: NPWT reduced ulcer volume by 60% vs 30% at 6 weeks; faster time to surgical closure (42 vs 68 days)
landmarkTrials.impact: NPWT is effective for Stage III-IV ulcers as a bridge to surgical closure; cost limits use in Kenya
landmarkTrials.reference: Zhu Z, et al. Negative pressure wound therapy for pressure ulcers: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Int Wound J. 2020;17(5):1330-1340.

evidenceTable.outcome: Ulcer size reduction 25-40% more than control

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: ASSESS RISK -> INSPECT -> GRADE -> TREAT -> PREVENT. ASSESS RISK: All hospitalized patients with limited mobility should undergo pressure ulcer risk assessment using a validated tool. The Braden Scale (6 subscales: sensory perception, moisture, activity, mobility, nutrition, friction/shear) is the most widely validated. A Braden score <=18 indicates at-risk, and <=12 indicates high risk. In Kenyan wards where formal Braden scoring may not be performed, a simpler approach: any patient who is bedbound, incontinent, and malnourished is at high pressure ulcer risk and requires full preventive measures. INSPECT: Examine all bony prominences systematically: sacrum, coccyx, heels, trochanters, ischial tuberosities, occiput, scapulae, elbows, malleoli. In darkly pigmented skin, look for persistent color changes (red, blue, purple), warmth, induration, or edema rather than classic erythema — the 'blanching test' (fingertip pressure for 3 seconds) is less reliable in pigmented skin; consider using a transparent disk to assess blanching. Document the location, stage, size (length x width x depth in cm), presence of undermining/tunneling, wound bed characteristics (granulation, slough, eschar), exudate (type and amount), odor, surrounding skin condition (maceration, erythema, induration), and pain. GRADE: Use the NPUAP/EPUAP staging system (see pathophysiology section). Note that staging can only be applied after removing necrotic tissue — an eschar-covered ulcer is 'unstageable' until debrided. Deep tissue injury (purple/maroon intact skin or blood-filled blister) may progress to Stage III or IV as the necrotic tissue demarcates. TREAT based on stage: Stage I: pressure relief (reposition q2h, pressure-relieving surface), skin care (keep clean and dry, barrier creams). Stage II: as above plus protective dressings (hydrocolloid, foam, or transparent film). Stage III: as above plus wound debridement (autolytic, enzymatic, sharp, or surgical), wound dressings that manage exudate (alginates, foams, hydrofibers), and consider NPWT. Stage IV: as Stage III plus surgical consultation for debridement, flap reconstruction, or excision of underlying bony prominence; IV antibiotics if osteomyelitis or cellulitis present. Nutritional support is essential at all stages: target protein intake 1.5-2.0 g/kg/day, supplemented with arginine, zinc, and vitamin C. PREVENT: The key preventive interventions are: (1) repositioning every 2 hours (the '30-degree tilt' position rather than full lateral to reduce trochanteric pressure), (2) use of pressure-relieving surfaces (alternating pressure mattress or overlay), (3) keeping skin clean and dry (manage incontinence with pads or catheters, barrier creams), (4) nutritional optimization (high-protein diet, supplements), (5) heel protection (suspend heels off the bed surface using pillows or heel boots), and (6) staff and family education. In Kenya, family members are often the primary caregivers in the ward and should be trained in turning techniques.

managementPearls.pearl: Stage I pressure ulcers are REVERSIBLE with prompt pressure offloading. The window of opportunity is 24-48 hours. Once the skin becomes non-blanchable and purple, irreversible ischemic injury has occurred and will progress to tissue necrosis over 5-10 days.

In darkly pigmented skin, Stage I pressure ulcers may not present with visible erythema. The earliest signs are: warmth (compare to the contralateral side by the back of your hand), induration (firmness on palpation), edema (pitting or non-pitting), and patient-reported discomfort or burning sensation at the site. Train nursing staff to palpate bony prominences, not just inspect them.

The 30-degree tilt (propping the patient at a 30-degree angle to the bed surface using pillows) produces lower interface pressures over the trochanter than the full 90-degree lateral position. This simple positioning technique, when combined with q2h turning between supine, right tilt, and left tilt, is the standard of care for pressure ulcer prevention.

Heel ulcers are the most commonly preventable pressure ulcer. The heel should be 'suspended' (offloaded) using a pillow placed under the calf, not under the heel itself. Commercial heel protectors are helpful but not essential — a rolled towel under the distal calf accomplishes the same goal without cost.

A 'kiss ulcer' (contiguous ulcer on opposing skin surfaces, e.g., between the legs or between the buttocks) indicates prolonged immobility with the skin surfaces in contact. Separate the surfaces with a soft pad or dressing to prevent moisture accumulation and further breakdown.

operativeNuances.nuance: Surgical reconstruction for Stage IV pressure ulcers involves excision of the ulcer, underlying bursa, and bony prominence (partial ostectomy), followed by flap coverage. For sacral ulcers, the gluteus maximus myocutaneous advancement flap (or V-Y advancement flap) is the workhorse. For ischial ulcers, the hamstring V-Y advancement flap or inferior gluteal artery perforator flap is preferred. For trochanteric ulcers, the tensor fascia lata (TFL) flap or vastus lateralis flap. The key principles: excise the ulcer down to healthy bleeding tissue, remove the bony prominence (e.g., ischiectomy for ischial ulcers), and provide well-vascularized muscle and skin coverage. Postoperative management must include: no pressure on the flap for 6 weeks, a air-fluidized bed if available, and strict turning protocols. The recurrence rate after flap reconstruction is 15-30% at 2 years if the underlying risk factors (immobility, incontinence, malnutrition) are not addressed.

Debridement of pressure ulcers: sharp debridement with a scalpel or curette is the most efficient method for removing necrotic tissue, eschar, and slough. It can be performed at the bedside for superficial ulcers or in the operating room for deep, extensive ulcers. The debrided tissue should be cultured if infection is suspected. Enzymatic debridement (collagenase) is slower but useful for maintenance debridement between surgical procedures. Autolytic debridement using hydrocolloid or hydrogel dressings is suitable for clean wounds with minimal slough. Maggot debridement therapy (MDT) using sterile Lucilia sericata larvae is a low-cost, effective option for debriding necrotic pressure ulcers in resource-limited settings. MDT costs approximately KES 500-1,000 per treatment vs KES 5,000-15,000 for surgical debridement, making it a viable option in Kenya where larvae can be obtained from specialized laboratories.

prognosis.healingRate_StageI: 95% heal within 1-2 weeks with pressure offloading
prognosis.healingRate_StageII: 80% heal within 4-8 weeks with appropriate wound care
prognosis.healingRate_StageIII: 50-60% heal within 8-16 weeks; surgical closure often needed
prognosis.healingRate_StageIV: 30-40% heal within 16-24 weeks; flap reconstruction often needed
prognosis.complicationRate: Cellulitis: 10-20%; osteomyelitis: 5-15% (Stage IV); bacteremia/sepsis: 2-5%; mortality from sepsis: 10-20%
prognosis.recurrenceRate: 30-50% at 1 year without risk factor modification; 15-30% after flap reconstruction
prognosis.kenyanConsiderations: Higher Stage III-IV prevalence at presentation, limited access to pressure-relieving surfaces and NPWT, reliance on family caregivers for turning, high recurrence rates due to lack of home care support
prognosis.keyDeterminants: Stage at presentation, nutritional status (albumin >30 g/L associated with 3x faster healing), pressure offloading compliance, infection control, and caregiver training
